briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for July 23, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Extreme Weather Events and Disasters Threatening the World
sections.content: Over the past week, extreme abnormal climate phenomena have been observed across various regions of South Korea, with torrential rains equivalent to 'two years' worth of rainfall' [1, 14]. Concurrently, the first suspected heat-related death of the year occurred in Gyeongnam Province [54], and unpredictable weather patterns, including intermittent heatwaves and showers, are intensifying nationwide [56]. Internationally, France recorded over 5,700 excess deaths due to a June heatwave [92], and severe wildfires in Canada directly impacted citizens' lives by issuing air quality alerts in 20 U.S. states [94]. Wildfire situations are also worsening in Minnesota and Michigan [64, 67], and warnings that climate change is burning down homes are growing louder [74, 79].

sections.title: 🌍 Long-Term Repercussions and Social Costs of Climate Change
sections.content: Climate change is not merely a short-term weather anomaly but poses long-term, systemic threats. Research has reported that the Atlantic Ocean's current system has been disrupted due to global warming [10], and drought is intensifying in the Colorado region [58]. Studies are underway suggesting that climate change could reshape disease risk and spread, linking to various disease issues such as the spread of dengue fever in low- and middle-income countries [66] and difficulties in controlling cyclosporiasis [72] [59, 60]. Furthermore, millions in small island nations are facing increased food insecurity due to climate change [70, 76], and warnings have emerged that the list of uninsurable assets will grow [65]. Statements that Minnesota cities cannot bear the costs of climate change alone [69] indicate that the climate crisis is imposing immense financial burdens across society.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Carbon Neutrality Policies and Activating Citizen Action
sections.content: Domestically, policy efforts towards carbon neutrality are taking concrete shape. An amendment to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act has been approved by a subcommittee of the National Assembly's Special Committee on Climate Crisis, setting greenhouse gas reduction targets within the range of 69-80% by 2040 and stipulating reduction targets until 2049 in law [13, 17, 26, 27, 33, 40, 42]. The participation of local governments and civil society is also active: Siheung City launched a 'Climate Citizen Network' comprising 105 citizens to earnestly begin carbon neutrality practices [18, 21, 24, 31, 36], and Suwon Special City is installing rain gardens and rainwater pergolas and operating citizen participation programs as part of its carbon-neutral green city development project [23, 25]. The Korea Environmental Industry & Technology Institute (KEITI) has introduced, for the first time, a system allowing citizens to donate carbon neutrality points earned through activities like using tumblers to the Community Chest of Korea, encouraging green lifestyle practices [19, 34, 39, 41]. However, concerns about policy continuity have also been raised as Gyeonggi Province has halted the payment of 'Climate Action Opportunity Income' compensation [57].

sections.title: 🌱 Responding to the Climate Crisis Through Renewable Energy and Technological Innovation
sections.content: Innovative technological development and efforts to transition to renewable energy are also steadily continuing to address the climate crisis. Renewable energy startups are expanding their businesses, focusing on power generation using SAF (Sustainable Aviation Fuel) and agricultural byproducts [43], and KEPCO KDN is leading the activation of the distributed energy market through public-led solar power projects [50, 52, 53]. S-OIL is taking the lead in achieving carbon neutrality by commencing the supply of bio marine fuel [28], and companies like Lenovo and Volkswagen Group Africa are accelerating carbon-neutral manufacturing, with Lenovo transitioning 90% of its global operations' electricity to renewable energy and Volkswagen Group Africa expanding solar power generation [48, 51, 91]. Domestically, agricultural technologies adapting to climate change are also advancing, with the first distribution of 'Geumseon Potatoes,' specialized for drought and high temperatures, in Seosan [12, 16]. Danyang County is promoting the establishment of a carbon-neutral hub in the central region through on-site inspections of CCU (Carbon Capture, Utilization) industrial facilities [22].

briefing.closing: As we have reviewed in today's briefing, we have once again confirmed that the climate crisis is an urgent global issue that must be addressed through innovation and cooperation. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Emergency treatment for heatstroke patients during heatwave. Kyunghyang Shinmun archive photo. Gyeongnam region reported its first suspected heat-related illness death this year. According to Gyeongnam Province on the 23rd, a 97-year-old woman, Ms. A, was found collapsed by a neighbor in a greenhouse in Hail-myeon, Goseong-gun, around 1:22 PM the previous day. Ms. A was transported to the hospital but passed away around 2:01 PM that day. Gyeongnam Province presumes Ms. A's cause of death was collapsing while working in a small greenhouse near her home.

summary.phenomenon: On June 22nd, at approximately 1:22 PM, a 97-year-old woman was found collapsed in a greenhouse in Hail-myeon, Goseong-gun, Gyeongnam Province. She was transported to a hospital and passed away around 2:01 PM. This marks the first suspected heat-related illness death in the Gyeongnam region this year.
summary.cause: It is presumed that the deceased collapsed while working in a small greenhouse near her home.
